candidates.title: Absorption-Quality Causal Chain via Epidemiological Methods
candidates.summary: Resolve the width/L0 confound in the absorption-quality correlation (r=-0.595) using width-stratified partial correlations with L0 as covariate, formal mediation analysis, and Rosenbaum sensitivity bounds. First application of epidemiological causal methods to SAE evaluation. Combined with cross-domain absorption measurement and scaling surface for a comprehensive study.
hypotheses: H1b: Within-width correlations significant in >= 2/3 strata
candidates.pilot_focus: Step 1.1: Add log(L0) as covariate to existing 54-SAE partial correlation. Go if |r| > 0.2 for at least one quality metric.
strengths: Directly addresses iter_4 BLOCKING issue (highest ROI)
Zero GPU cost for the critical confound analysis
Novel methodological contribution (epidemiological methods for SAE evaluation)
Combined with cross-domain and scaling surface for comprehensive paper
Negative result (H1 falsified) is equally publishable and informative
weaknesses: 54-SAE dataset may have insufficient within-stratum sample size (n < 10 per width group)
Mediation analysis requires parametric assumptions that may not hold
Cross-domain probes may have insufficient quality on Gemma 2B
Scaling surface requires SAEBench precomputed data availability

candidates.title: Absorption Phase Diagram: Unified Scaling Laws for Feature Recovery Under Hierarchy
candidates.summary: Construct the first empirical phase diagram mapping absorption rate in (log(width), log(L0)) space, with theoretical scaffolding from compressed sensing phase transitions. Three predicted regimes: hedging (low W), absorption (intermediate W, low L0), and recovery (high W, high L0). Validated on 200+ SAEBench SAEs.
hypotheses: H3b: Absorption monotonically increases with width
H3c: Absorption decreases with L0 at fixed width
H3d: Gradient surface has at least one ridge
candidates.pilot_focus: Download SAEBench data, fit simple linear model of absorption on log(width) + log(L0). If R^2 > 0.3, the scaling surface is worth characterizing.
strengths: Purely data-driven (zero GPU if using precomputed SAEBench data)
No paper has produced a unified phase diagram of SAE failure modes
Immediately actionable for practitioners (hyperparameter guidance)
Theoretical framework (three regimes) provides interpretive scaffolding
weaknesses: Phase boundaries may be smooth, not sharp (overpromise risk)
Theoretical predictions may not match empirical surface
SAEBench data coverage may be uneven in parameter space
Single evaluation task (first-letter) for absorption metric

candidates.title: Cross-Domain Feature Absorption: From Spelling to Knowledge Hierarchies
candidates.summary: First measurement of absorption on knowledge-domain hierarchies (city-country, city-continent, city-language) using RAVEL data with probes trained on the correct model. Side-by-side comparison with first-letter absorption on the same SAEs. Correlation of absorption severity with hierarchy sharpness.
hypotheses: H2d: Absorption correlates with hierarchy MI
candidates.pilot_focus: Train city-country probe on Gemma 2B layer 12. If accuracy > 85%, measure absorption on 3 high-frequency + 3 low-frequency countries.
strengths: Fills explicitly identified gap (5+ papers call for cross-domain validation)
Existing infrastructure covers 80% of implementation
Negative result (no absorption on knowledge tasks) is equally valuable
Practical implications for safety (knowledge features are safety-relevant)
weaknesses: Probe quality may be insufficient on smaller models
Absorption metric thresholds may not transfer from spelling to knowledge

candidates.title: Feature Immunodominance: Predicting Absorption from Ecological Competition Theory
candidates.summary: Formalize absorption as competitive exclusion using Lotka-Volterra dynamics and immunodominance theory. R* rule predicts which features absorb which. Active suppression signature tests whether encoder weights learn inhibitory patterns. Epitope focusing explains why masked regularization works.
hypotheses: R* rule predicts absorption direction with AUROC > 0.70
Absorbed parents show negative encoder-direction correlation with absorber child
Immunodominance model outperforms single-variable baselines by AUROC >= 0.05
candidates.pilot_focus: Compute R* for features in one SAE. Test R* rule on known absorbed features from Chanin labels.
strengths: Deeply novel cross-domain contribution (zero prior work connecting immunodominance to SAE)
Concrete quantitative predictions (R*, active suppression signature)
Intervention strategy (epitope focusing) connects to existing mitigation work
Zero training cost (analysis of existing SAEs)
weaknesses: Risk of being decorative analogy rather than load-bearing theory
Active suppression may not exist (encoder may be purely passive)
R* rule may not outperform simpler predictors
Does not address the blocking confound or cross-domain issues

candidates.title: The Honest Audit: Absorption Is Less Severe, Less General, and Less Causal Than Believed
candidates.summary: Systematic challenge to absorption literature: L0 confound explains quality correlation, first-letter task overestimates absorption severity, taxonomy rate is inflated. Pure deflationary paper. Reserved as fallback if H1 AND H2 are both falsified.
candidates.pilot_focus: Same as front-runner pilot (Step 1.1). Activated only if both H1 and H2 are falsified.
strengths: Publishable negative result with high practical impact
Redirects community investment away from misdirected mitigation efforts
All analyses already planned in main proposal
weaknesses: Purely deflationary (may be perceived as destructive without constructive contribution)
Requires both H1 and H2 to fail (low probability ~10%)
The contrarian's extreme framing may alienate reviewers

selection_rationale: cand_causal_chain is the front-runner because it directly addresses the iter_4 BLOCKING issue (width/L0 confound) with the highest ROI (zero GPU for the critical analysis), produces a novel methodological contribution (epidemiological methods for SAE evaluation), and integrates cross-domain validation and scaling surface into a comprehensive paper. The go/no-go structure (Step 1.1 L0 covariate) ensures fast failure detection. The backup candidates are ordered by: cand_phase_diagram (zero GPU, novel visualization), cand_cross_domain (fills explicit literature gap), cand_immunodominance (high novelty but not blocking), cand_honest_audit (activated only if main hypotheses fail).
